items.prompt_md: Why can `held` never be updated directly from `sold` in the buy/sell-with-cooldown recurrence?
options: Because the cooldown rule requires a rest day between selling and buying again
Because `held` is always initialized to negative infinity
Because `sold` and `held` must always be equal
items.answer: Because the cooldown rule requires a rest day between selling and buying again
items.explanation_md: `held` can only be reached from `rest`, not from `sold` directly — that structurally forces one cooldown day between a sale and the next buy.

items.prompt_md: You're making a sequence of buy/sell decisions across days, where selling locks you out of buying again for one extra day. Which approach fits?
options: Greedily sell whenever tomorrow's price is lower
Track a small set of named states (holding, sold, resting) and compute the best value in each state at every day
Sort the prices and take the largest gaps
Use a sliding window of fixed size over the prices
items.answer: Track a small set of named states (holding, sold, resting) and compute the best value in each state at every day
items.explanation_md: A cooldown constraint that couples consecutive decisions is the fingerprint of state-machine DP: name the states, write the transition formulas, and evaluate them left to right.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the rolled-down state-machine solution for buy/sell with cooldown, given n days?
items.explanation_md: Each day's held/sold/rest values only depend on the previous day's values, so one linear pass suffices and only a handful of variables need to be kept.
